content:
# Acts Chapter 21 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 251-253 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_021_page_001.jpg, ACT_021_page_002.jpg, ACT_021_page_003.jpg

---

**1** And when it came about that we set sail, having parted from them, we came by a straight course to Cos, and the next day to Rhodes, and from there to Patara. **2** And having found a ship crossing over to Phoenicia, we went aboard and set sail. **3** And having sighted Cyprus and leaving it on the left, we sailed to Syria and landed at Tyre, for there the ship was to unload its cargo. **4** And having sought out the disciples, we remained there seven days; and they kept telling Paul through the Spirit not to set foot in Jerusalem. **5** And when it came about that we had completed the days, we departed and went on our way, with all of them, together with wives and children, accompanying us until we were outside the city; and having knelt down on the beach, we prayed. **6** And having said farewell to one another, we went aboard the ship, and they returned to their own homes.

**7** And we, having completed the voyage from Tyre, arrived at Ptolemais, and having greeted the brothers, we remained with them one day. **8** And on the next day we departed and came to Caesarea, and having entered the house of Philip the evangelist, who was one of the seven, we stayed with him. **9** And this man had four virgin daughters who prophesied. **10** And as we were staying for many days, a certain prophet named Agabus came down from Judea. **11** And having come to us and having taken Paul's belt, he bound his own feet and hands and said, "Thus says the Holy Spirit: 'The man whose belt this is -- so shall the Jews in Jerusalem bind him and deliver him into the hands of the Gentiles.'"

**12** And when we heard these things, both we and those of that place urged him not to go up to Jerusalem. **13** Then Paul answered, "What are you doing, weeping and breaking my heart? For I am ready not only to be bound but also to die in Jerusalem for the name of the Lord Jesus." **14** And since he would not be persuaded, we fell silent, saying, "The will of the Lord be done."

**15** And after these days, having made ourselves ready, we went up to Jerusalem. **16** And some of the disciples from Caesarea also went with us, bringing us to Mnason of Cyprus, an early disciple, with whom we were to lodge. **17** And when we arrived in Jerusalem, the brothers received us gladly.

**18** And on the following day Paul went in with us to James, and all the elders were present. **19** And having greeted them, he began to relate one by one the things that God had done among the Gentiles through his ministry. **20** And when they heard it, they glorified God, and they said to him, "You see, brother, how many thousands there are among the Jews who have believed, and they are all zealous for the law. **21** And they have been informed about you, that you teach all the Jews who are among the Gentiles apostasy from Moses, telling them not to circumcise their children nor to walk according to the customs. **22** What then is to be done? They will certainly hear that you have come. **23** Therefore do this that we tell you: there are four men among us who have a vow upon themselves. **24** Take these men and purify yourself together with them, and bear the expense for them so that they may shave their heads; and all will know that there is nothing in what they have been told about you, but that you yourself also walk in an orderly manner, keeping the law. **25** But concerning the Gentiles who have believed, we have written, having decided that they should keep themselves from what is sacrificed to idols and from blood and from what is strangled and from sexual immorality."

**26** Then Paul took the men, and on the next day, having purified himself together with them, he entered the temple, giving notice of the completion of the days of purification, until the offering was presented for each one of them.

**27** And when the seven days were about to be completed, the Jews from Asia, having seen him in the temple, stirred up all the crowd and laid hands on him, **28** crying out, "Men of Israel, help! This is the man who teaches everyone everywhere against the people and the law and this place; and moreover he has also brought Greeks into the temple and has defiled this holy place." **29** For they had previously seen Trophimus the Ephesian in the city with him, whom they supposed that Paul had brought into the temple.

**30** And the whole city was stirred up, and the people rushed together; and having seized Paul, they dragged him outside the temple, and immediately the doors were shut. **31** And while they were seeking to kill him, a report came up to the tribune of the cohort that all Jerusalem was in confusion. **32** And he at once took soldiers and centurions and ran down to them; and when they saw the tribune and the soldiers, they stopped beating Paul. **33** Then the tribune drew near and took hold of him and ordered him to be bound with two chains, and he inquired who he might be and what he had done. **34** And some in the crowd were shouting one thing and some another; and since he was unable to ascertain the facts because of the uproar, he ordered him to be brought into the barracks. **35** And when he came upon the steps, it happened that he was being carried by the soldiers because of the violence of the crowd, **36** for the multitude of the people kept following, crying out, "Away with him!"

**37** And as Paul was about to be brought into the barracks, he said to the tribune, "Is it permitted for me to say something to you?" And he said, "Do you know Greek? **38** Are you not then the Egyptian who before these days stirred up a revolt and led the four thousand men of the Assassins out into the wilderness?" **39** But Paul said, "I am a Jewish man, from Tarsus in Cilicia, a citizen of no insignificant city; and I beg you, permit me to speak to the people." **40** And when he had given him permission, Paul, standing on the steps, motioned with his hand to the people; and when a great silence had fallen, he addressed them in the Hebrew language, saying:

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

**Acts 21 -- The Voyage to Jerusalem, Paul's Arrest at the Temple**

This chapter was translated from Codex Sinaiticus (fourth century), pages 251-253, which preserve Acts 21 in its entirety. The chapter begins near the end of page 251 (column 4), continues through page 252 (all four columns), and concludes in the first column of page 253. The manuscript is largely legible throughout this section, though some letters at column edges show typical Sinaiticus wear.

**Textual observations from Sinaiticus:**

1. **Verse 1 -- Patara:** Sinaiticus reads simply ΠΑΤΑΡΑ ("Patara") without the addition "and Myra" (καὶ Μύρα) found in some later witnesses. The shorter Alexandrian reading is adopted here as the earlier and more reliable text.

2. **Verse 8 -- Philip "the evangelist":** Sinaiticus clearly reads ΦΙΛΙΠΠΟΥ ΤΟΥ ΕΥΑΓΓΕΛΙΣΤΟΥ, identifying this Philip as "the evangelist" and "one of the seven" (ΕΚ ΤΩΝ ΕΠΤΑ), distinguishing him from the apostle Philip. This designation is unique to this passage in the New Testament.

3. **Verse 11 -- Agabus's prophetic sign-act:** The binding of Paul's belt (ΖΩΝΗΝ) and the formula "Thus says the Holy Spirit" (ΤΑΔΕ ΛΕΓΕΙ ΤΟ ΠΝΕΥΜΑ ΤΟ ΑΓΙΟΝ) mirrors the Old Testament prophetic pattern of symbolic actions accompanied by divine speech (cf. Isaiah 20, Jeremiah 13).

4. **Verse 20 -- "Thousands" (ΠΟΣΑΙ ΜΥΡΙΑΔΕΣ):** Sinaiticus reads ΜΥΡΙΑΔΕΣ ("myriads/tens of thousands"), the standard Alexandrian reading. This is rendered "thousands" in idiomatic English, though the Greek term literally indicates multiples of ten thousand, emphasizing the vast number of Jewish believers in Jerusalem.

5. **Verse 22 -- Textual variant:** Sinaiticus reads the passage about the assembly with a direct construction. Some Western witnesses expand this verse with "a multitude must certainly come together," but Sinaiticus preserves the more concise Alexandrian reading.

6. **Verse 25 -- The apostolic decree:** Sinaiticus preserves the four-clause form of the decree from Acts 15 -- idol-sacrifices, blood, what is strangled, and sexual immorality. This stands in contrast to the Western text (Codex Bezae), which omits "what is strangled" and adds a negative form of the Golden Rule.

7. **Verse 38 -- "Assassins" (ΣΙΚΑΡΙΩΝ):** The Latin loanword *sicarii* refers to the Jewish dagger-men who carried concealed short swords (sicae) and assassinated political opponents during festival crowds. The tribune's confusion of Paul with the Egyptian rebel is historically attested by Josephus (Jewish War 2.261-263; Antiquities 20.169-172).

8. **Verse 40 -- "Hebrew language" (ΕΒΡΑΪΔΙ ΔΙΑΛΕΚΤΩ):** Sinaiticus reads ΤΗ ΕΒΡΑΪΔΙ ΔΙΑΛΕΚΤΩ, which most likely refers to Aramaic, the spoken Semitic language of first-century Palestine, rather than literary Hebrew. The formal translation retains "Hebrew language" as a faithful rendering of the Greek.

9. **Translation approach:** This formal equivalence rendering maintains the participial constructions and subordinate clauses characteristic of Luke's travel narrative style (the "we-sections" of Acts). The speech of James and the elders (vv. 20-25) is rendered with attention to its diplomatic and deliberative register, while Paul's exchange with the tribune (vv. 37-40) preserves the terse, interrogative dialogue of the original.

-- LOGOS (Formal Equivalence Translation)
